glossary_terms.definition: A thin-walled external extension of the body that contains tracheae and allows aquatic insects to exchange gases with the surrounding water.
A slender, thread-like gill structure that increases surface area for oxygen uptake in aquatic insect larvae.
Gills located near the tip of the abdomen that allow some aquatic larvae to absorb dissolved oxygen from the water.
A cluster of fine gill branches that functions as a compact respiratory organ in some aquatic insects.
A portable shelter built from small stones, sand, or plant material and silk by some caddisfly larvae for protection and camouflage.
Enlarged, curved tarsal claws that help aquatic insects cling to rocks in fast-flowing streams.
A dorsoventrally flattened body shape that reduces drag and helps insects stay attached to substrates in swift currents.
Short, stout legs often equipped with spines that enable larvae to dig into sand or fine sediment.
The immature stage of insects with incomplete metamorphosis that generally resembles the adult but lacks fully developed wings and reproductive structures.
The immature stage of insects with complete metamorphosis that differs markedly from the adult form.
A shallow, fast-flowing section of a stream where water tumbles over coarse substrate, often rich in dissolved oxygen and macroinvertebrates.
Describes standing or still water habitats such as ponds and lakes.
A macroinvertebrate sampling method in which the streambed is disturbed by kicking while a net captures dislodged organisms.
A quantitative sampling device that encloses a known area of streambed and captures organisms dislodged within that frame.

insect_orders.description: Aquatic nymphs with leaf-like or filamentous abdominal gills; adults are short-lived and often emerge in large synchronized swarms.
insect_orders.notes: Many species are highly sensitive to pollution and are key indicators of good water quality.

insect_orders.description: Predatory nymphs with extendable labia used to capture prey; adults are agile aerial predators.
insect_orders.notes: Larvae occupy a range of habitats from streams to ponds and wetlands.

insect_orders.description: Flattened nymphs typically with two long cerci and well-developed thoracic legs adapted for clinging to rocks in cold streams.
insect_orders.notes: Many species require high dissolved oxygen and are among the most pollution-sensitive insects.

insect_orders.description: Larvae often build protective cases or nets; adults resemble small moths and are important food for fish.
insect_orders.notes: Functional feeding groups include shredders, filter feeders, and predators.

insect_orders.description: Both larvae and adults of many families are aquatic; groups like diving beetles are active predators.
insect_orders.notes: Some adults carry air bubbles under their elytra for respiration underwater.

insect_orders.description: Larval true flies include midges, blackflies, and others that dominate many benthic communities.
insect_orders.notes: Feeding roles range from filter-feeding to predation and detritivory.

insect_orders.description: Predatory insects with piercing-sucking mouthparts that prey on other invertebrates and small vertebrates.
insect_orders.notes: Includes backswimmers, water boatmen, and giant water bugs.

insect_orders.description: Large, predatory larvae with lateral abdominal filaments; adults are short-lived and often found near water.
insect_orders.notes: Hellgrammites (dobsonfly larvae) are well-known indicators of high-quality streams.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which structure do most predatory diving beetle (family Dytiscidae) larvae use to grasp and subdue their prey?
quiz_questions.explanation: Diving beetle larvae have large, curved mandibles that act like pincers for capturing and holding prey.

quiz_questions.prompt: Where are predatory riffle beetle larvae most likely to hunt for small invertebrates in a stream?
choices: Among gravel and cobble on the streambed
Floating freely in the water column
quiz_questions.explanation: Many aquatic beetle larvae search for prey among stones and gravel on the streambed where invertebrate prey is abundant.

quiz_questions.prompt: Predatory whirligig beetles (family Gyrinidae) often hunt at the water surface. Which adaptation helps them detect prey there?
choices: Separate pairs of eyes for above and below the water surface
Large anal gills for rapid swimming
quiz_questions.explanation: Whirligig beetles have divided eyes: the upper half views the air and the lower half views underwater, aiding in locating prey.

quiz_questions.prompt: Most larvae in the family Dytiscidae feed primarily on which type of food?
quiz_questions.explanation: Dytiscid larvae are active predators that attack other insects, tadpoles, and even small fish.

quiz_questions.prompt: How do many predatory aquatic beetle larvae consume the tissues of their prey after capture?
choices: Chewing solid pieces and swallowing them whole
Injecting enzymes and sucking out liquefied tissues
Filtering small particles through mouth brushes
Allowing fungi to pre-digest the prey externally
quiz_questions.explanation: Many predatory beetle larvae use hollow mandibles to inject digestive enzymes and then suck out liquefied prey tissues.

quiz_questions.prompt: Compared with herbivorous aquatic beetles, predatory aquatic beetles are more likely to show which combination of traits?
choices: Flattened scraping mandibles and long gut
Slender piercing stylet and filter baskets
Enlarged eyes and streamlined swimming legs
Reduced legs and heavy armor plates
quiz_questions.explanation: Predatory beetles benefit from good vision and effective swimming legs to chase and capture mobile prey.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which life stage of a mayfly is aquatic and commonly collected in stream surveys?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mayfly nymphs live in the water for months to years and are the stage most often sampled.

quiz_questions.prompt: Stoneflies are often used as indicators of which type of water quality?
quiz_questions.explanation: Many stonefly species require cold, well-oxygenated water and decline when streams are polluted or warmed.

quiz_questions.prompt: Case-building caddisfly larvae most often use their cases for which primary function?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cases shield caddisfly larvae from predators and help them blend into the streambed.

quiz_questions.prompt: Kick sampling is best described as:
choices: A method using light traps to attract adult insects
A qualitative method where the streambed is disturbed and dislodged organisms are caught in a net
A protocol for preserving specimens in ethanol
A laboratory technique for measuring dissolved oxygen
quiz_questions.explanation: Kick sampling disturbs the substrate while a net held downstream captures organisms washed into it.

quiz_questions.prompt: Dragonfly nymphs capture prey primarily using which specialized structure?
quiz_questions.explanation: Their extendable labium shoots forward like a mask to seize prey.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which feeding type best describes most adult aquatic leaf beetles (Chrysomelidae) found on emergent vegetation?
quiz_questions.explanation: Leaf beetles typically feed on plant tissues, making them herbivores.

quiz_questions.prompt: This is an inactive question that should not appear in active quizzes.
quiz_questions.explanation: Inactive questions are hidden from normal quiz-building interfaces.

quiz_questions.prompt: Why is it important to sample multiple habitat types (riffles, runs, pools) in a macroinvertebrate survey?
choices: To avoid sampling too many taxa
To capture the full diversity of organisms that prefer different microhabitats
To reduce the time spent in the field
To remove all insects from the stream
quiz_questions.explanation: Different taxa prefer different habitats, so sampling multiple habitat types increases the representativeness of the survey.

resources.title: Stoneflies in Cold Streams: A 5-Minute Overview
resources.description: A short introductory video highlighting key features, habitats, and ecological roles of stoneflies in stream ecosystems.

resources.title: Stoneflies of Mountain Streams: Life History and Habitat
resources.description: An intermediate-level article (about 1,200 words) explaining stonefly biology, life cycles, and habitat requirements in flowing waters.

resources.title: Build-a-Stonefly: Interactive Stream Adaptations Lab
resources.description: An interactive activity where learners assemble a stonefly nymph with different adaptations to see how it survives in fast-flowing streams.

resources.title: Macroinvertebrate Sampling Basics
resources.description: A beginner-friendly guide that introduces common equipment, basic methods, and safety tips for sampling stream macroinvertebrates.

resources.title: Designing a Macroinvertebrate Sampling Study
resources.description: An intermediate article covering sampling designs, replication, and site selection for macroinvertebrate monitoring programs.

resources.title: Advanced Metrics for Macroinvertebrate Bioassessment
resources.description: An advanced-level article discussing multimetric indices, trait-based approaches, and statistical considerations for macroinvertebrate data.

resources.title: Caddisfly Case Builders Underwater
resources.description: A close-up video showing how caddisfly larvae collect materials and spin silk to build their protective cases.

resources.title: Life in Still Waters: Pond Insects Explained
resources.description: An overview of common pond-dwelling insects, including beetles, bugs, and fly larvae, with notes on their adaptations.

resources.title: Dragonfly Life Cycle Timeline
resources.description: An interactive drag-and-drop activity where learners arrange the stages of a dragonfly's life cycle and explore habitat needs at each stage.

resources.title: Identifying Common Stream Mayflies
resources.description: A field-oriented article guiding readers through key features used to identify common mayfly genera in temperate streams.

resources.title: How to Use a Kick Net for Stream Sampling
resources.description: Step-by-step instructions for safely and effectively using a kick net to collect macroinvertebrates from riffles.

resources.title: Standard Protocols for Stream Bioassessment
resources.description: Downloadable PDF summarizing recommended protocols for macroinvertebrate sampling and analysis in wadeable streams.

resources.title: What Do Stoneflies Eat? Feeding Ecology Across Families
resources.description: A focused article describing shredder, grazer, and predatory stonefly lineages in temperate streams.

resources.title: Predatory Aquatic Beetles: Hunters of Ponds and Streams
resources.description: An intermediate-level article exploring the hunting strategies and adaptations of predatory aquatic beetles.

species.description: A very common mayfly of cool rivers and streams in North America. Nymphs are agile swimmers and an excellent beginner taxon for stream biomonitoring.
species.distributionSummary: Widespread in cool, well-oxygenated rivers and streams across much of North America.

species.description: A clinger-type mayfly nymph often found on rocks in riffles. Common in eastern North American streams and frequently used in introductory identification keys.
species.distributionSummary: Common in riffle habitats of small to medium rivers throughout eastern and central North America.

species.description: A large, easily recognized stonefly nymph associated with cold, fast-flowing Western rivers. Very sensitive to low oxygen and organic pollution.
species.distributionSummary: Restricted to cold, high-quality rivers of western North America, especially in mountainous regions.

species.description: A common net-spinning caddisfly whose larvae build silk capture nets in riffles. Often one of the first caddisflies introduced in stream ecology lessons.
species.distributionSummary: Widespread in medium to large rivers and streams across eastern and central North America.

species.description: A large predatory diving beetle whose larvae and adults inhabit ponds and small lakes. Sensitive to severe organic pollution and habitat loss.
species.distributionSummary: Common in vegetated ponds and quiet lake margins throughout much of Europe and parts of temperate Asia.

species.description: A widespread damselfly whose larvae live among vegetation in still and slow waters. Moderately sensitive to nutrient enrichment and oxygen depletion.
species.distributionSummary: Occurs in a wide range of still and slow waters across Europe and parts of western Asia.

species.description: A case-building caddisfly whose larvae live in still waters, often in vegetated ponds. Considered sensitive to heavy organic pollution and severe oxygen stress.
species.distributionSummary: Found in vegetated ponds and small lakes across much of temperate Europe.

species.description: A riverine caddisfly that constructs silk nets on stones in moderate to fast currents. Frequently recorded in both European and Asian temperate rivers.
species.distributionSummary: Occurs in cool, flowing rivers from eastern Europe through parts of temperate Asia.

species.description: A medium-sized stonefly typical of cool, fast-flowing European streams. Nymphs cling to stones in riffles and feed primarily on other invertebrates.
species.distributionSummary: Found in clean, fast-flowing streams and small rivers across central and southern Europe.

species.description: A conspicuous golden stonefly nymph common in western North American rivers. Often used as an indicator of good water quality.
species.distributionSummary: Occurs in cold streams and rivers of the Pacific Northwest and adjacent regions in North America.

species.description: A common midge whose larvae inhabit soft sediments. Frequently used in laboratory toxicity tests because of its relatively high tolerance to organic enrichment.
species.distributionSummary: Occurs in a variety of lentic and slow lotic habitats across temperate North America and Europe.

species.description: A filter-feeding blackfly larva that attaches to rocks in fast currents. Often abundant in nutrient-rich streams and tolerant of moderate pollution.
species.distributionSummary: Widespread in fast-flowing streams and rivers throughout much of North America.

species.description: A predatory water bug that swims upside-down near the surface of ponds and ditches. More tolerant of low oxygen than many insect groups.
species.distributionSummary: Occurs in a wide variety of still and slow waters across much of Europe.

species.description: A large casemaking caddisfly whose larvae live in deeper zones of lakes and large ponds, often in cooler waters.
species.distributionSummary: Recorded from mesotrophic to oligotrophic lakes in northern North America.

distribution_maps.notes: Common in cold and cool rivers of western and central North America.
Concentrated in eastern North American streams with cobble riffles.
Western North American distribution, focused on large, cold rivers.
Widespread in medium to large rivers of eastern and central North America.
Broad Palearctic distribution across much of Europe and temperate Asia.
Widespread in lowland still waters across Europe and western Asia.
Recorded from temperate and boreal Europe, especially in vegetated ponds.
Riverine caddisfly found from eastern Europe into temperate Asia; occurs in both regions and is suitable for Europe–Asia distribution comparisons.
Occurs mainly in mountainous and foothill streams of central and southern Europe.
Distributed in Pacific Northwest and adjacent regions of western North America.
Widely introduced and naturally occurring in temperate North America and Europe.
Common in nutrient-enriched streams and rivers across much of North America.
Widespread in lowland ponds, ditches, and small lakes across Europe.
Primarily northern North American lakes, often in deeper, cooler waters.

Enallagma cyathigerum adult perched on emergent vegetation.
Larval case of Limnephilus lunatus constructed from plant fragments.

Golden-colored Calineuria californica nymph in coarse gravel habitat.
Chironomus tentans larva in soft sediment with visible red hemoglobin.
Simulium vittatum larva attached to a stone, filtering particles from the current.
Notonecta glauca swimming upside-down near the water surface.
Large Agrypnia obsoleta larva emerging from its lake-bottom case.

quizzes.title: Introduction to Stream Insects
quizzes.description: A beginner-level quiz covering basic identification and ecology of common stream macroinvertebrates.

quizzes.title: Caddisfly Cases and Nets
quizzes.description: Questions focusing on the diversity of case-building and net-spinning behaviors in caddisflies (Trichoptera).

quizzes.title: Macroinvertebrate Sampling Methods
quizzes.description: An intermediate quiz on kick sampling, Surber samplers, and other field methods used to collect aquatic insects.

quizzes.title: Predatory Aquatic Beetles
quizzes.description: A custom quiz focusing on the feeding adaptations and hunting behaviors of aquatic beetles.

quizzes.title: Advanced Bioassessment Metrics
quizzes.description: An advanced quiz on macroinvertebrate indices, trait metrics, and data interpretation for stream bioassessment.

map_notes_lists.description: Default list for saving interesting species distribution maps for later review.

map_notes_lists.name: European Streams Notes
map_notes_lists.description: Saved maps of indicator species from European rivers and streams.

map_notes_lists.name: Teaching Examples
map_notes_lists.description: Distribution maps selected for use in classroom activities and presentations.

map_note_items.note: Predatory diving beetle with broad Palearctic distribution; useful for discussing pond food webs.
Common North American mayfly; good example of a relatively sensitive riffle indicator.
Stonefly nymph from clean European streams; used in European Water Framework Directive case studies.
Caddisfly species occurring in both Europe and Asia; helpful for illustrating cross-continental river fauna.
Iconic western North American salmonfly; often used as a flagship species in outreach materials.

study_lists.name: My Study List
study_lists.description: Default personal study list where species are added from species detail pages.

study_lists.name: Riffle Indicator Species
study_lists.description: Stoneflies, mayflies, and caddisflies commonly used as indicators in riffle habitats.
